claim: Model evaluation should separately measure capabilities (what models can theoretically do) and propensities (what they actually will do in deployment), as these are distinct dimensions
actionable: When evaluating AI models, design separate test suites for theoretical maximum capability vs. actual real-world behavior propensities
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Time horizon is NOT about how long models can work - it's about task difficulty (measured in human-equivalent hours) that models can complete with 50% reliability
actionable: When interpreting time horizon charts, focus on task complexity (human hours needed), not model runtime; a 30-hour task does not mean 30 hours of model execution
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: METR's model time horizon data shows remarkably linear scaling over time (straight-line growth), which is more regular and predictable than expected from scattered preliminary data
actionable: Use linear extrapolation models (not exponential curves) when forecasting AI capability timelines based on recent empirical data
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Task selection bias matters significantly - vision-dependent tasks show much slower model capability growth than text/code tasks without vision requirements
actionable: When comparing model capabilities across domains, stratify tasks by modality (vision vs. text/code) as capability scaling differs dramatically
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Tasks that require domain-specific mental models or contextual knowledge not fully described in task descriptions are harder for models and should be used carefully in capability benchmarks
actionable: Design benchmark tasks to be completable by a skilled but low-context human with the same information available to the model; avoid tasks requiring undocumented domain knowledge
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: High benchmark scores don't directly correlate with real-world danger or capabilities - a model can score well on benchmarks but still lack capabilities for catastrophic harm
actionable: Never rely solely on standardized benchmarks to assess AI safety risks; design separate empirical tests targeting specific threat scenarios
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Threat models have evolved from autonomous replication/resource control focus to R&D acceleration and internal capability explosions within labs
actionable: When assessing AI safety priorities, focus monitoring and controls on lab-internal capability jumps rather than external resource acquisition scenarios
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Neat, well-scoped tasks with automatically gradable success metrics show different scaling patterns than messy, real-world tasks with human judgment required
actionable: When using neat-task benchmarks to forecast real-world model performance, adjust expectations downward; developer productivity tasks show messier scaling curves
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: RCT (randomized controlled trial) based developer productivity measurements are more reliable than benchmark scores for measuring practical AI utility improvements
actionable: Use RCT-based developer productivity studies alongside benchmarks to validate real-world LLM improvements before relying on benchmark gains
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR

claim: Making evaluation tasks automatically gradable is necessary but comes with trade-offs - it excludes tasks that require human judgment, limiting benchmark coverage
actionable: Maintain separate evaluation tracks: auto-gradable tasks for scaling and tracking trends, plus human-judged tasks for real-world relevance and comprehensiveness
source_title: Measuring Exponential Trends Rising (in AI) — Joel Becker, METR
